techniques.evidence_quote: the initial compromise of the client was a Gitlab vulnerability (CVE-2021-22205). The attacker exploited the vulnerability in Gitlab, and gained access to sensitive data, which included the access key for an Admin level identity in the victims AWS environment.
The attackers initial access into the AWS environment was a ListBuckets that came through this access key from the Indonesian IP address 182.1.229.252
The attacker leveraged the stolen access key to CreateUser named backup and CreateAccessKey via S3 Browser.
The attacker leveraged the stolen access key to CreateUser named backup and CreateAccessKey via S3 Browser.
The attacker than initiated a PutUserPolicy to create a policy named backupuser which allowed full privileges to all resources for the identity backup
Armed with the new identity backup the attacker now began enumeration efforts. The attacker logged into AWS console with the newly created backup identity
gained access to sensitive data, which included the access key for an Admin level identity in the victims AWS environment

Services explored by the attacker included: health.amazonaws.com, support.amazonaws.com, ec2.amazonaws.com, elasticloadbalancing.amazonaws.com, s3.amazonaws.com, cloudfront.amazonaws.com, trustedadvisor.amazonaws.com, monitoring.amazonaws.com, compute-optimizer.amazonaws.com
The attackers initial access into the AWS environment was a ListBuckets that came through this access key
About thirty-one (31) minutes after initial access, the attacker began to use the AWS web console to create EC2 instances for the purpose of crypto mining.

attack_chain.description: Attacker exploited GitLab CVE-2021-22205, a remote code execution vulnerability in the victim's GitLab instance, to gain access to sensitive files stored on the system.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the initial compromise of the client was a Gitlab vulnerability (CVE-2021-22205). The attacker exploited the vulnerability in Gitlab, and gained access to sensitive data

attack_chain.technique_name: Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API
attack_chain.description: Attacker retrieved AWS Admin-level IAM access keys stored in sensitive data exposed by the GitLab vulnerability.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: gained access to sensitive data, which included the access key for an Admin level identity in the victims AWS environment

attack_chain.description: Attacker used the stolen Admin-level AWS access key from an Indonesian IP address with S3 Browser user-agent to authenticate into the AWS environment and called ListBuckets.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attackers initial access into the AWS environment was a ListBuckets that came through this access key from the Indonesian IP address 182.1.229.252 with a User-Agent of S3 Browser 9.5.5

attack_chain.description: Attacker issued a ListBuckets API call to enumerate all S3 buckets in the AWS environment using the stolen Admin access key.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attackers initial access into the AWS environment was a ListBuckets that came through this access key

attack_chain.description: After a failed attempt to PutUserPolicy on an existing backup identity (due to an invalid resource ARN template left in the policy), the attacker created a new IAM user named 'backup' and generated an access key for it.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker leveraged the stolen access key to CreateUser named backup and CreateAccessKey via S3 Browser.

attack_chain.description: Attacker created a new IAM user named 'backup' with an inline policy ('backupuser') granting full wildcard permissions (Action: *, Resource: *) to maintain persistent privileged access.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attacker than initiated a PutUserPolicy to create a policy named backupuser which allowed full privileges to all resources for the identity backup

attack_chain.description: Using both the original stolen key and the new 'backup' identity, the attacker logged into the AWS console and enumerated a broad range of AWS services including EC2, ELB, S3, CloudFront, CloudWatch, and others.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Services explored by the attacker included: health.amazonaws.com, support.amazonaws.com, ec2.amazonaws.com, elasticloadbalancing.amazonaws.com, s3.amazonaws.com, cloudfront.amazonaws.com, trustedadvisor.amazonaws.com, monitoring.amazonaws.com, compute-optimizer.amazonaws.com

attack_chain.technique_name: Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure: Create Cloud Instance
attack_chain.description: Approximately 31 minutes after initial access, the attacker used the AWS EC2 launch wizard to create an SSH key pair, a security group with port 22 open to 0.0.0.0/0, and attempted to launch dozens of xlarge EC2 instances across multiple regions for cryptomining. 13 instances were successfully created across 5 regions. All instances had CloudWatch detailed monitoring disabled.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: About thirty-one (31) minutes after initial access, the attacker began to use the AWS web console to create EC2 instances for the purpose of crypto mining. Using the AWS EC2 launch wizard the attacker would CreateKeyPair and CreateSecurityGroup

attack_chain.description: Attacker successfully launched 13 xlarge EC2 instances across 5 AWS regions to perform cryptocurrency mining, exploiting the victim's cloud compute resources.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: In total the attacker successfully created thirteen (13) ec2 instances in five (5) different regions... the attacker began to use the AWS web console to create EC2 instances for the purpose of crypto mining.

initial_access_vector: Exploitation of GitLab CVE-2021-22205 to extract AWS Admin-level IAM access keys stored on the GitLab server
persistence_mechanism: Created new IAM user 'backup' with an access key and a wildcard inline policy (Action: *, Resource: *) using the stolen Admin credentials
privilege_escalation: Attacker already had Admin-level keys; created new IAM user 'backup' with full wildcard permissions via PutUserPolicy to ensure continued privileged access
